terms.definition: A technique used by AI models to solve complex problems by breaking them down into a sequence of logical intermediate steps, effectively 'thinking out loud' before giving a final answer.
terms.analogy: In a kitchen, it is the difference between a chef simply handing you a finished cake versus a chef who narrates every step—'first I cream the butter, then I fold in the flour'—to ensure the logic of the recipe is followed correctly.

terms.definition: AI systems that are integrated into a physical body (like a robot) or a simulated 3D environment, allowing them to perceive and interact with the world rather than just processing text or images on a screen.
terms.analogy: A standard GPS is just software that tells you where to go, but a self-driving car is an 'embodied agent' because it has 'eyes' (sensors) and 'limbs' (wheels and steering) to actually move through and impact the physical world.

terms.definition: A framework where multiple AI programs, or 'agents,' work together, communicate, and coordinate their actions to solve tasks that are too complex for a single AI to handle alone.
terms.analogy: Think of a professional restaurant kitchen: instead of one person trying to cook everything, you have a specialized team—a head chef, a pastry chef, and a line cook—all communicating in real-time to get a complex five-course dinner to the table.
